summary: Cadastral census register — a systematic count of individuals (kiki = self, x11) organized by geographic zones within a walled compound (kaqa = wall-AY, x2), marked by boundaries (taqa) and oriented east (paqa). 171 cords, 5 colors, 28 clusters, 1-PAIRE. Header declares subject in 3 grammatical forms (ki-PASS, ki-DIR, TOP-ki) with totals 42 and 24. Body counts in pairs (value 2 dominant), BG=1 (individuals), MB=4 (subtotals). Closes on chiqa (truth, cord 171, BG) = verified. Museo Amano, Lima. 21 STRING, 100% coverage.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  171 cords (L1: 167, L2: 4). 21 STRING (12%, 100% hits), 129 INT, 21 EMPTY.
  Architecture: 1-PAIRE (header: 3 paired L1+L2). 28 clusters, 5 colors: AB(79), W(48), MB(22), BG(13), PK(6).
  Museum: Museo Amano, Lima (R4248). Provenance: Unknown.
  OKR: Beginning knot has colored threads (light brown, light blue, red). Markers ~1cm. Yellow camelid markers on cords 165-166.

KEY VOCABULARY (onset-corrected)
  kiki x11 (chiki->kiki, self/individual worker) — IDENTITY, DOMINANT
  chiqa x3 (truth/verification) — GOVERNANCE
  ki x3 (chi->ki, possessive/abbreviated kiki) — GRAMMAR
  kaqa x2 (wall-AY) — GEOGRAPHY
  taqa x1 (boundary) — GEOGRAPHY
  paqa x1 (dawn/east) — GEOGRAPHY

STRUCTURE
  Header (cords 1-6): Paradigmatic declaration ki-PASS, ki-DIR, TOP-ki + totals 42 and 24.
  First cadastral section (cords 7-80): kaqa opens it. 14 sub-sections of mostly-2 values.
  Second section (cords 81-127): kaqa opens. kiki appears regularly (cords 90-122).
  Geographic closing (cords 128-133): taqa (boundary) + paqa (east) + chiqa (truth).
  Final register (cords 134-171): Sparse, more kiki entries. Last cord = chiqa on BG.
  Value pattern: AB=2 (pairs), BG=1 (individuals), MB=4 (subtotals).

NARRATIVE — THE CENSUS OF SELVES
  Self. That is the word that structures this khipu — *kiki*, the individual, the mit'a worker counted for the census. It appears eleven times across 171 cords, each time marking a specific person or person-level entry in a register that otherwise counts in pairs.
  The document opens with a declaration. Three paired cords carry the word *ki* in three grammatical cases — passive, directional, topical — like a title page: 'Concerning the counted selves.' Two numbers follow: 42 and 24. The totals.
  Then the walls. *Kaqa* (cord 7) — the Aymara word for a stone wall, because this is the coast where both languages are spoken. The wall marks the first zone. Inside it, the scribe counts: 2, 2, 2, 2, 2. Almost every cord carries the value two — pairs. The color tells what kind: AB for subjects, W for data, MB for subtotals (value 4), BG for a single individual (value 1).
  A second wall at cord 81. The count begins fresh: 29 persons in this zone. Now the kiki entries begin: cords 90, 102, 110, 112, 113, 119, 122. Individual selves, each identified within the paired structure.
  At cord 128, the boundary: *taqa*. The edge of the surveyed area. Then *paqa* — dawn, east. And *chiqa* — truth. The survey is verified.
  The very last cord, 171, carries one word on beige thread: *chiqa*. Truth. The census is complete.
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Cadastral census analysis, onset correction chi->ki
Museo Amano, Lima — Yoshitaro Amano collection, central coast Peru
